narration: The elevator opened on an empty cabin. An invitation. Yusuf begged me to walk out the front door and never look back. But it would just keep ringing — for the next kid on the night desk, and the next — the way it rang for him. Somebody has to finish the call. So at three, I answered. Static. Then my own voice, saying words I hadn't said yet: room service for 408. I carried the tray up myself. The paint around the door was already broken. Inside, it's still 1994 — a phone off the hook on the nightstand. A bed turned down. A chair pulled out. For me. If you ever work nights at the Halden Grand: it's quiet at 3 AM now. Mostly. And there's a new rule on the card. Rule eight. If the phone asks for Deniz... tell them he checked out.

shots.prompt: At 3 AM sharp Deniz Aksoy (burgundy waistcoat) lifts the ringing switchboard receiver in the lamplight and holds it to his ear; his face slowly empties of fear as the tiny brass lamp pulses in rhythm like slow breathing; then he sets the receiver gently back down, decided. Ends on: his calm, resolved face in the lamplight.

Deniz Aksoy (burgundy waistcoat) lifts a silver-cloched room-service tray from the reception counter; Yusuf Kara (navy porter uniform) blocks his path for a heartbeat, hands half-raised in plea — then steps aside, head bowing, unable to watch. Ends on: Deniz walking past him with the tray, steady.

Deniz Aksoy (burgundy waistcoat) stands centered in the vintage elevator holding the silver tray level; the brass cage doors fold shut across him; above, the floor-indicator arrow climbs smooth and unhurried toward the fourth-floor mark. Ends on: the arrow arriving at the fourth-floor mark with a soft settle.

The door of room 408 stands ajar for the first time, warm golden light pouring through the crack, the old paint seal split along the frame; Deniz Aksoy (burgundy waistcoat) balances the tray on one arm and pushes the door slowly open with his fingertips, face bathed in the glow. Ends on: him stepping into the light as the door swallows him.

The fourth-floor corridor afterwards: the dead sconces relight one by one down its length; the far door stands closed, its paint seal smooth and unbroken as if never opened, the room-service tray gone, the 'Do Not Disturb' card hanging perfectly still. Slow pull-back down the empty corridor. Ends on: the corridor at rest, long and silent.

Pale dawn light in the lobby: Yusuf Kara (navy porter uniform) stands behind the reception counter in a clerk's posture and writes one careful line in the logbook, closes the leather cover, and places a small brass name tag on top of it like a headstone; behind him the switchboard's tiny lamp blinks once, softly, and goes dark. Ends on: the name tag resting on the closed logbook in the morning light.
